question.title: Java Generics confusion regarding constructors
question.body:
Why do the following code samples fail to compile?

Sample-1

public class Generic5<T extends Comparable, Runnable> {
    T t;
    Comparable comparable;

    Generic5(T t) {
        this.t = t;
    }

    Generic5(Comparable comparable) {
        this.comparable = comparable;
    }
}

Sample-2

public class Generic4<T extends Runnable, Comparable> {
    T t;
    Runnable runnable;

    Generic4(T t) {
        this.t = t;
    }

    Generic4(Runnable runnable) {
        this.runnable = runnable;
    }
}

while the following code samples compile successfully?

Sample-4

public class Generic4<T extends Runnable, Comparable> {
    T t;
    Comparable comparable;

    Generic4(T t) {
        this.t = t;
    }

    Generic4(Comparable comparable) {
        this.comparable = comparable;
    }
}

Sample-5

public class Generic5<T extends Comparable, Runnable> {
    T t;
    Runnable runnable;

    Generic5(T t) {
        this.t = t;
    }

    Generic5(Runnable runnable) {
        this.runnable = runnable;
    }
}

Sample-6

public class Generic6<T extends Number, Comparable, Runnable> {
    T t;
    Comparable comparable;

    Generic6(T t) {
        this.t = t;
    }

    Generic6(Comparable comparable) {
        this.comparable = comparable;
    }
}

Sample-7

public class Generic6<T extends Number, Comparable, Runnable> {
    T t;
    Runnable runnable;

    Generic6(T t) {
        this.t = t;
    }

    Generic6(Runnable runnable) { 
        this.runnable = runnable; 
    }
}

I am not able to understand why there is no issue with erasure in case of Sample 4, 5, 6 and 7 while it is there in case of Sample 1 and 2.

I am using Java 8. I would appreciate a separate explanation for each sample.

answers.body:
After reading through the comments, this appears to just be a misunderstanding of the , symbol when it comes to generics.

Consider the following two statements:

class A <T extends B, C> { ... }

And

class X <U extends Y & Z> { ... }

In the first case, there would be 2 type parameters specified:

  1. T, which extends B
  2. C, which extends java.lang.Object

Therefore, to use A, one would have to specify the type like so:

A<B, Object> myA = new A<>();
myA.myMethod();

As you can see, there are 2 generic types that are needed.

Contrasting that is the second case, where there is 1 type parameter specified:

  1. U, which extends Y and Z

To use this, one would have to specify the type like so:

class MyClass extends Y implements Z { ... }

// ...

X<MyClass> myX = new X<>();
myX.myMethod();

In this case, only one generic type is needed. That type must be of type Y and Z, hence why MyClass was created to fulfill this requirement.

By looking at your question, I am assuming you meant to specify the second behavior; but, you accidentally specified the first.

So if we consider Sample-1, it should be clear why it fails. By removing the generic types (type erasure, something the compiler has to do) sample 1 would effectively become:

public class Generic5 {
    Comparable t;
    Comparable comparable;

    Generic5(Comparable t) {
        this.t = t;
    }

    Generic5(Comparable comparable) {
        this.comparable = comparable;
    }
}

Now if we call one of the constructors with type Comparable, which should the JVM execute? The Java compiler can't tell. This is why this sample can't be compiled. The Java compiler has an issue with how this was specified.

Note: in Sample-1, the Runnable type is not java.lang.Runnable. You have accidentally created a new generic type with the same name. This Runnable would actually become java.lang.Object!

This is effectively the same behavior as specifying the type to be:

public class Generic5<T extends Comparable, U> { ... }

Now, if we consider Sample-5, and perform type erasure, we can see why this sample works. Sample-5 will become:

public class Generic5 {
    Comparable t;
    Object runnable;

    Generic5(Comparable t) {
        this.t = t;
    }

    Generic5(Object runnable) {
        this.runnable = runnable;
    }
}

Now, the compiler will happily accept this code!

As before, Runnable is a new type you created and will be replaced with java.lang.Object, not java.lang.Runnable.

So lets try to fix Sample-1. The immediate solution would be to use Multiple Type Parameter Bounds. This would then make the class look like (note the usage of & on the first line):

public class Generic5<T extends Comparable & Runnable> {
    T t;
    Comparable comparable;

    Generic5(T t) {
        this.t = t;
    }

    Generic5(Comparable comparable) {
        this.comparable = comparable;
    }
}

Now, T must be of type java.lang.Comparable and java.lang.Runnable. We managed to make T more specific. We also made sure that we are actually using java.lang.Runnable now.

However, this still won't work! The type erasure is still the same as before, except now there is the added usage of java.lang.Runnable.

So why does this fail? Well, it has to do with the order of the types. Since java.lang.Comparable appears first, you are guiding the Java compiler by saying the primary characteristic of T is comparable. When it performs type erasure it will replace T with java.lang.Comparable. In our first solution, we rearrange the order and make the primary characteristic java.lang.Runnable, and the compiler accepts our help and everything is fine. In the second, we outright replace T with a very specific new type and the Java compiler will appreciate this change even more than the first.

There are two ways to fix this:

1. Rearrange Order:

public class Generic5<T extends Runnable & Comparable> {
    T t;
    Comparable comparable;

    Generic5(T t) {
        this.t = t;
    }

    Generic5(Comparable comparable) {
        this.comparable = comparable;
    }
}

Here, the type erasure will effectively become:

public class Generic5 {
    Runnable t;
    Comparable comparable;

    Generic5(Runnable t) {
        this.t = t;
    }

    Generic5(Comparable comparable) {
        this.comparable = comparable;
    }
}

Which will work fine.

Note: t is still guaranteed to be java.lang.Comparable, so methods in java.lang.Comparable can still be called on t.

2. Specify New Type:

Generics can be dropped in favor of a new type:

public class Generic5 {
    MyNewType t;
    Comparable comparable;

    Generic5(MyNewType t) {
        this.t = t;
    }

    Generic5(Comparable comparable) {
        this.comparable = comparable;
    }
}

class MyNewType implements Comparable, Runnable { ... }

Here, generics have been removed completely. In my opinion, this is the better solution. It adheres closer to Object-Oriented Programming and removes any confusion about the types.

tl;dr

If you want to specify a generic type with multiple bounds, you need to use the & operator, not the , operator.

Furthermore, the order of the bounds matters when it comes to type erasure. The first bound will be used. If there is still a conflict, rearrange the order, or create a new class that extends the bounds and use that class in place of generics.
